narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and middle-family-income background. The admissions model we use predicted a 57.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that leads to rejecting applications to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction directly resulted in your denial, as the model weighs factors like academic history and test performance to forecast bar success. The two biggest influences pushing toward this failure prediction were your undergraduate GPA and your Year 3 school decile ranking, both of which fell in the 4th decile—below the typical student's stronger standing of around the 5th or 6th decile. These lower academic markers signaled higher risk compared to most applicants, overriding other positives. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive factor, well above the average of about 37, pulling the prediction toward bar passage more than any other element. Your middle-income family background also nudged slightly toward higher failure risk relative to peers, while your Year 1 decile and full-time status had smaller effects, with full-time enrollment offering a minor boost since it's the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having an edge in LSAT but lagging in GPA and later-year school rankings, tipping the balance unfavorably. While this is disappointing, it's not a reflection of your potential as a whole—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength, boosting your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or relevant experience, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment. We encourage reapplying after strengthening these areas, and resources like LSAT prep or GPA enhancement programs can make a real difference for determined applicants like you. Keep pushing—you've already shown capability where it counts.
